statement.markdown: Correctly implement quick sort algorithm to sort the input array in ascending order. Quick sort works by selecting a 'pivot' element from the array and partitioning the other elements into two sub-arrays, according to whether they are less than or greater than the pivot. The sub-arrays are then recursively sorted.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be an array of integers. The size of the array will be between 1 and 10000.
statement.outputFormat: Sort the input array in ascending order.
statement.notes: Your implementation should be efficient enough to handle arrays of size 10000.

hints.text: Choose a good pivot selection method.
Base case for recursion: when the length of the array is 1 or less, return the array.
In the recursive call to sort the array, make sure to return the sorted array.

editorial.title: Correct Implementation of Quick Sort
editorial.intuition: Quick sort works by selecting a 'pivot' element from the array and partitioning the other elements into two sub-arrays, according to whether they are less than or greater than the pivot. The sub-arrays are then recursively sorted.
editorial.bruteForce: Quick sort is not a brute force solution. It uses Divide and Conquer technique.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use the Lomuto partition scheme for quick sort.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Quick sort is a correct algorithm for sorting an array of integers. The proof of correctness is based on the following properties: The recursive calls to sort the sub-arrays are made on arrays of size less than n. The sub-arrays are sorted recursively. The pivot element is always placed in its final position.
editorial.dryRun: Let's take an example array: [9, 3, 1, 5, 7]. Choose a pivot, say 5. Partition the array: [1, 3, 9] and [7]. Recursively sort the sub-arrays and then combine them.

functionSignature.cpp: void quickSort(int arr[], int low, int high);
functionSignature.java: public void quickSort(int[] arr, int low, int high);
